topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printout lay crumpled on the kitchen counter, the stark lettering of the genetic markers mocking me. My hands were shaking so violently I had to grip the edge of the granite countertop to steady them. Every line, every percentage, felt like a verdict delivered by an unseen judge. I stared at the results, my breath catching in shallow, ragged gasps. How could this be? The quiet hum of the refrigerator suddenly seemed deafening, amplifying the frantic thumping in my chest. I pushed the paper away, the sound of the crinkling paper sounding like tearing fabric. I needed an answer, a solid anchor in this swirling chaos, but all I had was this cold, undeniable truth staring back at me.

[story 2]

I paced the length of the living room floor, my footsteps echoing hollowly in the silence. Each stride was heavy, propelled by a churning nausea that settled deep in my stomach. The DNA report sat on the coffee table, a physical manifestation of a sudden, terrifying void. I ran a hand through my hair, pulling at strands as if to physically pull the confusion out of my skull. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy. I forced myself to sit down on the sofa, the springs groaning beneath my weight. My gaze kept flicking to the window, watching the indifferent streetlights, searching for some sign that this whole revelation was a cruel trick, a misunderstanding. The air felt thick, suffocating, and I couldn't draw a full, steady breath.

[story 3]

The fluorescent light in the sterile clinic room seemed to burn directly into my eyes. I kept re-reading the summary, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. A cold sweat broke out across my forehead, soaking the collar of my shirt. My jaw was clenched so tight I could feel the ache in my molars. I looked down at my own hands, flexing my fingers repeatedly, searching for some evidence that I was wrong, that the data was flawed. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat—a sound of pure, uncontained distress. I needed to confront someone, to demand clarification, but the words caught in my throat, lodged there like shards of glass. I just sat there, utterly still, waiting for the next wave to crash over me.
